title: Listening - Part 4: Extreme Sports
description: You will hear five short extracts in which people talk about their involvement in extreme sports. You must complete two tasks while listening.

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I started base jumping after years of skydiving. [21]I think I just needed to prove to myself that I could handle the ultimate adrenaline rush without a safety net[/21]. It was about self-mastery, really. People think we're crazy, but it's very calculated. Interestingly, [26]it's helped me stay incredibly calm during high-pressure negotiations at work[/26]. When you've faced a mountain cliff, a boardroom meeting seems pretty insignificant. || Speaker 2: For me, ultra-marathon running wasn't about the medals. [22]I was going through a messy divorce and needed something to focus my mind on entirely[/22]. The physical pain was a distraction from the emotional stuff. It saved my sanity. Now, [27]I’ve noticed I have a much higher threshold for physical discomfort in everyday life[/27]. Things like a cold house or a long walk with heavy bags just don't bother me anymore. || Speaker 3: I took up free-diving because [23]my doctor suggested I find a way to manage my chronic anxiety[/23]. I needed to learn how to breathe and control my heart rate. It’s been transformative. But the real bonus was [28]the incredible sense of community I found at the local club[/28]. I’m quite shy, so having a group of people who share this niche passion has given me a social life I never expected to have. || Speaker 4: Big wave surfing was always the dream. [24]I think I was mostly trying to live up to my father's reputation in the surfing world[/24]; he was a legend in the 80s. I felt I had a lot to justify. It’s been a tough road, but [29]I’ve gained a profound respect for the power of the natural environment[/29]. You realise very quickly that you're not in charge; the ocean is. It makes you feel very small, in a good way. || Speaker 5: I got into ice climbing almost by accident during a trip to Norway. [25]I’ve always been fascinated by the technical gear and the physics involved in the sport[/25]. I love the kit. What’s been the most rewarding, though, is [30]the way it’s forced me to improve my time-management and organisational skills[/30]. You can't be messy or late when you're on a glacier; everything has to be perfectly planned, and that's spilled over into my professional life too.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21 – 25, choose from the list (A – H) the speaker's main reason for taking up the sport.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions wanting to prove they could handle the rush and 'self-mastery'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 used the sport as a 'distraction' from a messy divorce.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 started on medical advice to manage 'chronic anxiety'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to 'live up to my father's reputation'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 is 'fascinated by the technical gear and the physics'.

options.C: to satisfy a curiosity about equipment
options.E: to cope with a difficult life event

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26 – 30, choose from the list (A – H) what each speaker has gained from the experience.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 says it helped them 'stay incredibly calm' at work.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions a 'higher threshold for physical discomfort'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 found an 'incredible sense of community'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 gained a 'profound respect for the power of the natural environment'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 improved their 'organisational skills' and planning.
